summary: DeVault demonstrates a creative hack combining Cage, a new kiosk-mode Wayland compositor, with wlroots' RDP backend to create a seamless remote desktop experience. He shows how running Cage on a laptop with FreeRDP connecting to a remote sway session on a desktop produces an experience nearly identical to running sway locally. The post serves as both a congratulations to Cage creator Jente Hidskes and a practical demonstration of the composability of wlroots-based tools. DeVault acknowledges this isn't true network transparency but highlights it as a cool demonstration of what's possible with the Wayland ecosystem.
key_points: Cage is a minimal Wayland compositor that runs a single fullscreen application and exits when it closes, originally designed for kiosks
Combining Cage with wlroots' new RDP backend and FreeRDP creates a seamless remote desktop session that feels like running sway locally
The composability of wlroots-based tools enables creative use cases that weren't explicitly designed for
The hack isn't true network transparency but demonstrates the flexibility of the Wayland ecosystem's modular architecture
money_quotes: Cage is a Wayland compositor designed for kiosks - though, as you'll shortly find out, is useful in many unexpected ways.
This lets you basically add a DRM+KMS+libinput session to any Wayland-compatible application and run it in a tiny wlroots compositor.

The result is an experience which is basically identical to running sway directly on my laptop, except it's actually running on my desktop and using the remote desktop protocol to send everything back and forth.
This isn't especially practical, but it is a cool hack.
It's definitely not network transparency like some people want, but I wasn't aiming for that.
key_insight: The modular design of wlroots enables creative compositions of small tools like Cage and RDP backends to produce surprisingly seamless experiences that weren't explicitly designed for.
